query: arch space creation for second and third molar eruption mechanisms

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Technique or Treatment -- Before the Eruption of the Permanent Mandibular First Molar
retrieved_docs.content: Even if there are no adjacent teeth, operators should cautiously measure the available mesiodistal dimension, and precise proximal tooth reduction should be performed to prevent unnecessary crown margin overhang. This is particularly pertinent before the first permanent molar erupts distally to the second primary molar (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown in Primary Second Molar). If there is any encroachment in the space required for the eruption of the first permanent molar, it leads to a distorted eruption pathway of the permanent molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Tooth Eruption Theories
retrieved_docs.content: Various mechanisms have been proposed to explain tooth eruption. Marks and Schroeder introduced criteria for a valid tooth eruption theory, emphasizing 3 fundamental eruption facts: [3] Teeth move axially and in 3 dimensions. Teeth exhibit distinct eruption characteristics and have varying stage-specific needs. The functional position of teeth is inheritable. A concise overview of historical and contemporary tooth eruption theories is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Intraosseous eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The initial phases of tooth eruption commence when the crown fully develops and root formation begins. [4] In this phase, an eruption pathway is created through bone resorption that facilitates the tooth's emergence. [4] Studies on dog premolars have revealed that the role of the dental follicle in tooth eruption is pivotal, as it ensures timely eruption and exfoliation of the tooth, irrespective of the crown's influence. Although earlier it was proposed that pressure from the developing tooth triggers osteoclastic activity and forms the eruption pathway, [4] this study disproves that theory. The study is outlined as follows: Alveolar resorption occlusal to the tooth persisted despite immobilizing the tooth bud by ligating the dog's mandible. [5] The unerupted tooth's crown was extracted and substituted with a metallic replica, which was subsequently inserted into the dental follicle, resulting in the normal eruption of the tooth. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth impaction may affect anyone, with incidence rates varying from 5.6% to 18.8%, and it tends to be more common in female individuals. The frequency of tooth impaction follows a pattern opposite to the typical chronological eruption sequence. Teeth that erupt later are more likely to be impacted due to insufficient arch space. The mandibular 3rd molar is the most commonly impacted tooth, followed by the maxillary 3rd molar, maxillary canine, mandibular premolars, maxillary premolars, and 2nd molar. The teeth with the lowest impaction rates are the mandibular incisors, 1st molars, and primary teeth. Impaction of these teeth is relatively rare and is typically associated with specific conditions, such as retained deciduous teeth or abnormalities like an odontoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Impacted teeth characteristically remain under the gingival tissue and do not erupt into their ideal position. Impacted teeth can be a significant cause of malocclusion, leading to the shifting of adjacent teeth. When impacted teeth do not erupt into the dental arch, additional space exists for adjacent teeth to shift into, which will serve as an obstacle for if and when the impacted tooth may erupt later. Impacted third molars are often experienced during adolescence or early adulthood and may cause interferences in occlusion if not extracted or exposed. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Eruption Patterns
retrieved_docs.content: The first permanent teeth that emerge in the oral cavity are the maxillary and mandibular first molars (see Image. First Sign of Right Incisor Breakthrough). These teeth typically emerge around age 6 and are called 6-year molars. These molars erupt distal to the deciduous second molars and are accompanied or preceded by the exfoliation of the deciduous mandibular central incisors. The permanent mandibular incisors typically erupt in individuals aged 6 to 7, followed by the permanent maxillary incisors between the ages of 7 and 9. The permanent anterior teeth develop lingual or palatal to the deciduous teeth. In contrast, the permanent premolars grow in the spaces between and beneath the roots of the deciduous molars they replace.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- The equilibrium theory
retrieved_docs.content: Once the tooth reaches its functional plane, an additional eruption is stimulated by the vertical growth of the lower jaw, which moves away from the maxilla. With the expanding available space, the tooth continues to erupt, maintaining appropriate occlusal contact with the corresponding teeth in the opposing arch.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Two crucial factors aid in the process of tooth eruption. First, the permanent teeth require the removal of bone and primary roots. Second, soft tissue alterations are necessary, followed by the application of force that propels the tooth toward the occlusal surface, directing it along an established eruption pathway. [4] Despite significant progress, the exact mechanisms regulating these events are still not fully comprehended. Nonetheless, valuable insights have been gleaned from studies involving various species and clinical experiences.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Mandibular Third Molars -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lower anterior crowding is not an indication of extraction of lower third molars. Crowding of the lower anterior teeth is sometimes observed simultaneously with the eruption of lower wisdom teeth. This has led to the belief that the erupting third molars cause a mesial migration of teeth, consequently moving the incisors, resulting in crowding. [11] Several studies have investigated this hypothesis. Still, most have not found a cause-effect relationship between anterior crowding and the eruption of lower third molars. [11] Consequently, Zawawi & Melis (2014) argue that extraction of lower third molars does not alleviate anterior crowding and therefore does not justify the potential risk of damage to IAN. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Loss of permanent teeth and hypodontia can shift an otherwise ideal occlusion. Hypodontia may be caused by trauma or lack of tooth development. For instance, the loss of a maxillary tooth can allow the eruption of the opposing mandibular tooth into the opposing edentulous space. The adjacent teeth may also tip or shift to the empty space. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Post-occlusal eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism underlying horizontal movements remains a subject of investigation. One hypothesis suggests that horizontal displacements, such as buccal movement, occur through a coordinated process involving bone resorption, formation, and cementum apposition. [3] Cementum apposition may also be partly responsible for the compensatory eruption observed in tooth wear. [3] Teeth can continue to undergo eruption even in adulthood as a compensatory response to the loss of an opposing tooth, demonstrating that eruption activity persists throughout an individual's life. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Mucosal penetration and eruption
retrieved_docs.content: The movement responsible for elevating the tooth from the gingival to the occlusal plane is primarily attributed to the collagen fibers within the periodontal ligament. Collagen maturation, characterized by a cross-linking and shortening process, is pivotal in generating the propulsive force that drives the eruptive mechanism. [4] Oral forces can modify the orientation and quantity of these fibers, triggering the activation of this eruptive mechanism. [4] Medications that disrupt collagen maturation also hinder tooth eruption, strongly indicating that the collagen fibers within the periodontal ligament serve as the primary mechanism for post-emergent axial movement of the tooth. [4] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth eruption is the movement of the developing tooth from its position in the alveolar bone to the occlusal plane. For a tooth to erupt, it has to remove the alveolar bone and primary roots in its pathway and, later, the soft tissues. A propulsive force that moves the tooth in an occlusal direction along this eruption pathway is also required. Bone resorption occlusal to the unerupted tooth is believed to create an eruption pathway first. Then, bone apposition apical to the tooth generates propulsive occlusal movements, following the path. Various theories have been described to explain the physiology of dental eruption. The most widely accepted theory advocates that the dental follicle regulates bone remodeling mechanisms necessary for translocating the tooth through the eruption pathway to the alveolar crest. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The periodontal ligament is believed to mediate supraosseous events during tooth eruption. [1] In contrast, intraosseous events, which involve changes within the bone itself, are not directly influenced or regulated by the actions of the periodontal ligament. [1] These events primarily involve bone remodeling mechanisms and the creation of the eruption pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Occlusion is the term for how teeth fit together and interlock. In proper occlusion, the maxillary arch and teeth sit slightly anterior to the mandibular arch and teeth. In a transverse dimension, the maxillary teeth sit more laterally than the mandibular teeth. Correct occlusion is essential for the primary function of teeth, as a tight meshwork allows for efficient mastication. When this fitment is not ideal, it is called malocclusion, discussed in a later section.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Structure and Function -- Bite forces theory
retrieved_docs.content: Recent theories suggest that the soft tissues within the dental follicle can detect bite forces, which are believed to have a crucial role in directing bone remodeling processes and influencing tooth eruption. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: Asphyxiation and strangulation are medical events that are classified under the category of other injuries and conditions due to external causes, indicating that they arise from external mechanisms rather than internal disease processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2417
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious process is identified as a pathological mechanism underlying post‑abortion complications, and these complications are referred to in Arabic as “إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى.” This relationship highlights that infection can be a key contributor to adverse outcomes following an abortion, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing infectious etiologies to prevent or treat post‑abortion complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior repair of vagina is a surgical intervention that directly involves the vagina, aiming to restore its structural integrity. Vesicovaginal fistula with involvement of urinary continence mechanism following normal delivery is a pathological condition that is located in the vagina, often compromising urinary continence after childbirth. Both concepts underscore the clinical importance of the vagina as a site for therapeutic repair and as a potential location of obstetric complications affecting urinary function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Vesicovaginal fistula with involvement of urinary continence mechanism following normal delivery is a condition that is found in the vagina.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as occurring due to verlies; tanden door ongeval, indicating that loss of teeth caused by an accident can precipitate such contact. The phrase “Due to” is used to associate both verlies; tanden door ongeval and crushing injury or internal injury, showing that these conditions are commonly cited as causal factors. Additionally, crushing injury or internal injury is linked with إصابة, the Arabic term for injury, underscoring that these mechanisms represent forms of bodily injury. Together, these relationships illustrate how dental loss from accidents, crushing forces, and internal trauma are interrelated causes of accidental physical contact and overall injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: generic unspecified abnormal‑finding categories, all of which fall within the larger umbrella of injuries and conditions arising from external causes, including toxic exposures such as heavy metals and physical mechanisms such as asphyxiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.
